standards.name: Classifier Foundations
standards.description: Students know the classifier system: the handshape inventory, classifier types and groupings, recognizing classifiers in input, and the noun-first rule.

targets.statement: I can identify classifier handshapes and what they represent.

rubric.4: I can identify an expanded set of classifier handshapes across taught topics, and sort classifiers into taught types and groupings.
rubric.3: I can identify the common starter set of classifier handshapes and what each typically represents (people, vehicles, flat objects).
rubric.2: I can identify some starter classifier handshapes, but I mix up what several of them represent.
rubric.1: I can match classifier handshapes to what they represent only with a chart or word bank in front of me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t match a classifier handshape to what it represents even with a chart.

rubric.4: I can identify an extensive inventory of classifier handshapes and choose which types and groupings fit different kinds of referents.
rubric.3: I can identify an expanded set of classifier handshapes across taught topics, and I can sort classifiers into taught types and groupings (entity, handling, property).
rubric.2: I can identify the starter set of classifier handshapes and what each represents — but sorting classifiers into types and groupings isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can sort classifiers into types only with a chart, examples, or guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t match a classifier handshape to what it represents even with a chart.

rubric.4: I know the classifier system well enough to explain it: given any referent, I can name several handshape options, the types they belong to, and which choice fits which situation.
rubric.3: I can identify an extensive inventory of classifier handshapes, and I can choose which types and groupings fit different kinds of referents.
rubric.2: I can identify an expanded set of handshapes and sort them into taught types — but my inventory has gaps, and matching types to unusual referents trips me up.
rubric.1: I can work with the classifier inventory only with a chart or a walk-through.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t match a classifier handshape to what it represents even with a chart.

targets.statement: I can recognize classifiers in signed input and connect them to referents.

rubric.4: I can recognize classifiers in signed descriptions at a steady pace, connecting them to their referents through short scenes.
rubric.3: I can recognize a classifier in slow, clearly signed input and connect it to its referent when the referent has just been named.
rubric.2: I can sometimes spot that a handshape is working as a classifier, but I lose what it refers to.
rubric.1: I can recognize a classifier only when the signing is replayed slowly and the referent is pointed out.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t spot classifiers in signing even with replays.

rubric.4: I can recognize classifiers in signed input at a normal pace, connecting each to its referent even in scenes with several classifiers at work.
rubric.3: I can recognize classifiers in signed descriptions at a steady pace, connecting them to their referents through short scenes.
rubric.2: I can recognize a classifier in slow, clear signing when the referent was just named — but through a whole scene at a steady pace, I lose the connections.
rubric.1: I can follow classifiers in a description only with replays or slowed signing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t spot classifiers in signing even with replays.

rubric.4: I pick out and read classifiers automatically in any signing — fast narratives, several classifiers layered in one scene — without ever losing a referent.
rubric.3: I can recognize classifiers in signed input at a normal pace, connecting each to its referent even in scenes with several classifiers at work.
rubric.2: I can follow classifiers through short scenes at a steady pace — but at a normal pace, or with several classifiers working at once, connections drop.
rubric.1: I can track classifiers in normal-pace signing only with replays or coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t spot classifiers in signing even with replays.

targets.statement: I can name the referent before using a classifier.

rubric.4: I can introduce referents before classifying them consistently in my own sentences, though the habit may slip when I’m signing spontaneously.
rubric.3: I can name the referent before using a classifier to represent it in practiced sentences.
rubric.2: I can use noun-first in practiced sentences when reminded, but the classifier often comes out before I’ve named what it stands for.
rubric.1: I can follow the noun-first rule only when prompted each time.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my classifiers appear without referents even with prompting.

rubric.4: I can introduce referents before classifying them automatically in my own signing.
rubric.3: I can introduce referents before classifying them consistently in my own sentences, though the habit may slip in spontaneous signing.
rubric.2: I can name the referent first in practiced sentences — but in my own sentences, the habit slips often.
rubric.1: I can keep noun-first only with frequent reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my classifiers appear without referents even with prompting.

rubric.4: Noun-first is invisible habit for me, even in fast, complex storytelling — and I skillfully re-introduce referents when scenes get crowded.
rubric.3: I can introduce referents before classifying them automatically in my own signing.
rubric.2: I can keep noun-first consistent in my own sentences — but in spontaneous signing, classifiers still sometimes show up before their referents.
rubric.1: I can keep noun-first only with reminders during my signing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my classifiers appear without referents even with prompting.

standards.name: Depicting with Classifiers
standards.description: Students depict referents with classifiers: selecting the right classifier, building spatial layouts with reference points, describing size and shape, showing movement with descriptive detail, and switching scales.

targets.statement: I can select the right classifier for what I’m depicting.

rubric.4: I can select correctly among taught classifiers for familiar referents, with only occasional type errors.
rubric.3: I can use taught classifiers with the referents they were taught for, in practiced sentences.
rubric.2: I can use a classifier when the situation is practiced, but I often grab a handshape that doesn’t fit the referent.
rubric.1: I can use a classifier only when someone models which handshape to use.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I avoid classifiers entirely even with a model.

rubric.4: I can select the right classifier and type for what I’m depicting, even in situations I haven’t practiced.
rubric.3: I can select correctly among taught classifiers for familiar referents, though I still make occasional type errors (like showing an entity when handling fits).
rubric.2: I can use taught classifiers with the referents they were taught for — but choosing correctly among several options for a new familiar referent isn’t reliable yet.
rubric.1: I can pick the right classifier only with a model or coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I avoid classifiers entirely even with a model.

rubric.4: My classifier choices are precise and creative everywhere — I pick the handshape and type a fluent signer would, even for referents I’ve never depicted.
rubric.3: I can select the right classifier and type for what I’m depicting, in situations I haven’t practiced — including whether to show the entity itself or the handling of it.
rubric.2: I can select correctly among taught classifiers for familiar referents — but in unpracticed situations, type errors come back.
rubric.1: I can choose classifiers in new situations only with prompting or examples.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I avoid classifiers entirely even with a model.

targets.statement: I can build spatial layouts with classifiers.

rubric.4: I can place a few referents in space using taught layouts, beginning to use reference points.
rubric.3: I can place one or two referents in space following a modeled layout.
rubric.2: I can put a referent somewhere in space, but my placement doesn’t follow the modeled layout or stay put.
rubric.1: I can place referents in space only while copying a model step by step.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t place referents in space even while copying.

rubric.4: I can build spatial layouts with classifiers, using reference points, with spatial relationships that are clear to the viewer.
rubric.3: I can place a few referents in space using taught layouts and begin using reference points, though my spatial relationships may be unclear at times.
rubric.2: I can place one or two referents following a modeled layout — but on my own, with more referents, the layout stops reading clearly.
rubric.1: I can lay out a scene only with a model or step-by-step coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t place referents in space even while copying.

rubric.4: I can build complex scenes — many referents, layered arrangements, distance and orientation exact — so clearly that a viewer could sketch the scene from my signing alone.
rubric.3: I can build spatial layouts with classifiers, using reference points, with the distance, orientation, and arrangement clear to the viewer.
rubric.2: I can place a few referents with taught layouts and reference points — but the spatial relationships still read unclearly at times.
rubric.1: I can make a layout clear only with planning time or coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t place referents in space even while copying.

targets.statement: I can describe size and shape with classifiers.

rubric.4: I can describe the size and shape of familiar objects recognizably through classifier depiction.
rubric.3: I can trace the basic sizes and shapes of objects as I was taught.
rubric.2: I can attempt a size-and-shape trace, but it often doesn’t match the object’s actual proportions or form.
rubric.1: I can trace a size or shape only while copying a model.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t trace sizes and shapes even while copying.

rubric.4: I can depict the size, shape, and physical properties of referents with precision.
rubric.3: I can describe the size and shape of familiar objects recognizably through classifier depiction.
rubric.2: I can trace basic sizes and shapes as taught — but my depictions of real objects aren’t always recognizable.
rubric.1: I can describe an object’s size and shape only with a model or prompts.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t trace sizes and shapes even while copying.

rubric.4: My size-and-shape work is vivid and exact — texture, proportion, and detail come through, and viewers can identify an object from my depiction alone.
rubric.3: I can depict the size, shape, and physical properties of referents with precision.
rubric.2: I can describe familiar objects recognizably — but precision in proportions, properties, and detail isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can depict properties precisely only with coaching or examples.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t trace sizes and shapes even while copying.

targets.statement: I can show movement with classifiers.

rubric.4: I can depict movement along clear paths, adding speed or manner one element at a time.
rubric.3: I can move a classifier along a simple path, adding a basic descriptive element (fast, slow, jerky) when it’s modeled.
rubric.2: I can move a classifier from one point to another, but the path is vague and descriptive elements drop out without a model.
rubric.1: I can show movement only while copying a modeled depiction.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show movement with a classifier even while copying.

rubric.4: I can depict movement with layered detail, showing path, speed, and manner together.
rubric.3: I can depict movement along clear paths, adding speed or manner one element at a time.
rubric.2: I can move a classifier along a simple path with a modeled descriptive element — but clear paths with speed or manner added on my own aren’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can add speed or manner to movement only when prompted.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show movement with a classifier even while copying.

rubric.4: I can choreograph motion — several referents moving at once, path, speed, and manner layered with non-manual intensity — so the movement itself tells the story.
rubric.3: I can depict movement with layered detail, showing path, speed, and manner together.
rubric.2: I can depict movement along clear paths with speed or manner added one at a time — but layering path, speed, and manner together isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can layer movement detail only with coaching or a model.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show movement with a classifier even while copying.

targets.statement: I can switch scales when depicting.

rubric.4: I can switch between scales as the scene requires — from showing a referent with my body to showing it with a classifier — choosing the scale that serves the scene.
rubric.3: I can use two scales in taught depictions — showing a referent with my body in one moment and with a classifier in another — switching when the material calls for it.
rubric.2: I can show a referent with my body or with a classifier, but I stay locked in one scale rather than switching.
rubric.1: I can switch scales only when the switch is modeled and prompted.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show a referent at two scales even with a model.

rubric.4: I move between body scale and classifier scale fluidly and strategically — zooming in and out mid-scene the way Deaf storytellers do, with each switch making the scene clearer.
rubric.3: I can switch between scales as the scene requires — from representing a referent with my own body to showing it with a single classifier — choosing the scale that serves the scene.
rubric.2: I can use two scales in taught depictions and switch when the material calls for it — but choosing the right scale on my own, scene by scene, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can switch scales only with prompting or planning time.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show a referent at two scales even with a model.

standards.name: Blending Classifiers into Signing
standards.description: Students weave classifiers into connected language: moving between signs and depiction, showing referents interacting, and choosing the right depiction for the meaning.

targets.statement: I can blend classifiers into my sentences.

rubric.4: I can enter and exit classifier depiction within practiced sentence types, though I may pause or reset when shifting between signs and depiction.
rubric.3: I can produce practiced sentences that include a classifier alongside lexical signs, completing the sentence as one piece.
rubric.2: I can produce a classifier and a sentence, but they come out as separate performances rather than one piece.
rubric.1: I can include a classifier in a sentence only while copying a modeled sentence.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t put a classifier into a sentence even with a model.

rubric.4: I can move between lexical signs and classifier depiction within a sentence, entering and exiting depiction without breaking the flow of my signing.
rubric.3: I can enter and exit classifier depiction within practiced sentence types, though I may pause or reset when shifting between signs and depiction.
rubric.2: I can produce practiced sentences that include a classifier — but shifting between signs and depiction mid-sentence still throws me off.
rubric.1: I can blend depiction into a sentence only with a model or a planned script.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t put a classifier into a sentence even with a model.

rubric.4: Depiction and lexical signing are one seamless stream for me — I slip in and out of classifier work constantly, even in fast conversation, without a visible seam.
rubric.3: I can move between lexical signs and classifier depiction within a sentence, entering and exiting depiction without breaking the flow of my signing.
rubric.2: I can enter and exit depiction within practiced sentence types — but in my own signing, the shift still costs me a pause or a reset.
rubric.1: I can keep flow through a depiction shift only with rehearsal or coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t put a classifier into a sentence even with a model.

targets.statement: I can depict referents interacting.

rubric.4: I can depict two referents interacting in taught pairings, conveying the basic relationship between them.
rubric.3: I can depict one taught interaction between two referents (like one thing moving to another), conveying the basic idea.
rubric.2: I can show two referents, but the interaction between them doesn’t read clearly.
rubric.1: I can depict an interaction only while copying a model.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show two referents interacting even with a model.

rubric.4: I can depict referents interacting in any arrangement the meaning calls for, with the interaction clear to the viewer.
rubric.3: I can depict two referents interacting in taught pairings, conveying the basic relationship between them.
rubric.2: I can depict the one interaction I practiced — but new pairings of referents fall apart or read unclearly.
rubric.1: I can depict interactions only with a model or planned setup.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show two referents interacting even with a model.

rubric.4: I can stage multi-referent interactions of any complexity — collisions, chases, exchanges among three or more referents — with every relationship readable.
rubric.3: I can depict referents interacting in any arrangement the meaning calls for — one thing moving onto, past, or into another — with the interaction and relationship clear to the viewer.
rubric.2: I can depict taught pairings of referents clearly — but new arrangements still come out muddled.
rubric.1: I can make a new interaction clear only with planning or coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show two referents interacting even with a model.

targets.statement: I can choose depiction when it shows meaning best.

rubric.4: I can begin choosing classifier depiction on my own in familiar situations, selecting a depiction that fits the meaning.
rubric.3: I can select the correct classifier depiction for practiced situations, choosing among a small set of taught options.
rubric.2: I can use a depiction when told which one, but choosing among the taught options on my own isn’t reliable yet.
rubric.1: I can choose a depiction only when the options are laid out and someone helps me pick.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t match a depiction to a situation even with help.

rubric.4: I can choose classifier depiction wherever it shows something better than signs alone, selecting the depiction that conveys the meaning accurately.
rubric.3: I can begin choosing classifier depiction on my own in familiar situations — describing a scene, comparing objects — selecting a depiction that fits the meaning.
rubric.2: I can select the correct depiction for practiced situations from taught options — but choosing depiction on my own, unprompted, isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can choose to depict only when prompted that the situation calls for it.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t match a depiction to a situation even with help.

rubric.4: I judge sign-versus-depiction like an experienced signer — my choices consistently make meaning sharper, and I invent apt depictions for situations nothing in class prepared me for.
rubric.3: I can choose classifier depiction where it shows something better than signs alone, and I select the depiction that conveys the meaning accurately.
rubric.2: I can choose depiction on my own in familiar situations — but recognizing every spot where depiction beats signs, and picking the most accurate option, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can make the sign-versus-depiction call only with prompting.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t match a depiction to a situation even with help.

standards.name: History
standards.description: Students know, explain, and analyze the people and events of Deaf history.

targets.statement: I can tell who key figures in Deaf history were and why they matter.

rubric.4: I can explain why key figures in Deaf history were important and how they affected and interacted with other people and events.
rubric.3: I can identify key figures in Deaf history, telling who they were and generally why they’re important.
rubric.2: I can recognize the names of key figures, but I mix up who did what or why they matter.
rubric.1: I can identify key figures only with a word bank, notes, or guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify key figures even with a word bank and guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can analyze the contributions of key figures to Deaf culture on a deeper level, connecting readings and sources about them.
rubric.3: I can explain why key figures in Deaf history were important and how they affected and interacted with other people and events.
rubric.2: I can identify key figures and tell generally why they matter — but explaining how they affected and interacted with other people and events isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can explain a figure’s importance only with guiding questions or notes.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify key figures even with a word bank and guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can build an original argument about a figure’s legacy — synthesizing multiple sources, weighing conflicting accounts, and defending my interpretation.
rubric.3: I can analyze the contributions of key figures to Deaf culture on a deeper level, connecting readings and sources about them.
rubric.2: I can explain why key figures mattered and how they shaped events — but connecting multiple readings and sources into deeper analysis isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze a figure’s contributions only with guiding questions and sources picked for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify key figures even with a word bank and guiding questions.

targets.statement: I can work with the events of Deaf history.

rubric.4: I can match people to the events they shaped, expanding beyond the main timeline of Deaf history.
rubric.3: I can place the major events of Deaf history in order on a timeline.
rubric.2: I can name some major events, but their order on a timeline gets scrambled.
rubric.1: I can order events only with a word bank or a partially completed timeline.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t place events in order even with a word bank.

rubric.4: I can research a historical event or topic from multiple sides, comparing perspectives on it.
rubric.3: I can match people to the events they shaped, expanding my knowledge beyond the main Deaf history timeline.
rubric.2: I can place the major events in order on a timeline — but matching people to the events they shaped, and going beyond the main timeline, isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can match people to events only with notes or guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t place events in order even with a word bank.

rubric.4: I can run a genuine historical inquiry — finding sources beyond what’s assigned, weighing their reliability, and presenting an original comparison of perspectives.
rubric.3: I can research a historical event or topic from multiple sides, comparing the perspectives on it.
rubric.2: I can match people to events and work beyond the main timeline — but researching one topic from multiple sides, and comparing the perspectives, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can compare perspectives only with sources handed to me and guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t place events in order even with a word bank.

standards.name: Cultural Norms
standards.description: Students know, explain, and analyze Deaf cultural norms across three areas: technology, information sharing and community, and language use and visual access.

targets.statement: I can explain Deaf norms around technology.

rubric.4: I can explain how Deaf norms have been shaped by contact with technology, including examples of norms that changed as technology did.
rubric.3: I can identify core Deaf norms around technology and life without sound, stating the basic reason behind each.
rubric.2: I can name some norms around technology and life without sound, but not the reasons behind them.
rubric.1: I can identify these norms only with a word bank or guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can analyze how contact with technology has shaped and continues to reshape Deaf norms, including tensions between older and newer practices.
rubric.3: I can explain how Deaf norms have been shaped by contact with technology, including examples of norms that changed as technology did.
rubric.2: I can identify core technology norms and the basic reason behind each — but explaining how the norms changed as technology did isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can explain how technology shaped a norm only with guiding questions or examples laid out for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can take a position in the live debates (cochlear implants, video relay versus in-person interpreting) and argue it with evidence from Deaf perspectives, history, and my own analysis.
rubric.3: I can analyze how contact with technology has shaped and continues to reshape Deaf norms, including where tensions exist between older and newer practices.
rubric.2: I can explain how technology shaped Deaf norms with examples — but analyzing the ongoing reshaping, and the tensions between older and newer practices, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze technology-driven change only with guiding questions and sources picked for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

targets.statement: I can explain Deaf norms of information sharing and community.

rubric.4: I can explain how the norms of information sharing and community connection grew out of Deaf experiences and values.
rubric.3: I can identify core norms of information sharing and community connection, stating the basic reason behind each.
rubric.2: I can name some information-sharing and community norms, but not the reasons behind them.
rubric.1: I can identify these norms only with a word bank or guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can analyze the norms of information sharing and community connection, examining the experiences and values that produced them and how they’re changing.
rubric.3: I can explain how the norms of information sharing and community connection grew out of Deaf experiences and values.
rubric.2: I can identify the core norms and basic reasons — but explaining how they grew out of Deaf experiences and values isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can connect these norms to Deaf experiences only with guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can analyze how these norms are evolving right now — social media, dispersed communities — and make defensible predictions grounded in the values underneath.
rubric.3: I can analyze the norms of information sharing and community connection, examining the experiences and values that produced them and how they’re changing.
rubric.2: I can explain how these norms grew out of Deaf experiences and values — but analyzing how they’re changing isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze these norms only with guiding questions and sources picked for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

targets.statement: I can explain Deaf norms of language use and visual access.

rubric.4: I can explain how the norms of language use and visual access grew out of the visual nature of the Deaf community.
rubric.3: I can identify core norms of language use and visual access, stating the basic reason behind each.
rubric.2: I can name some language-use and visual-access norms, but not the reasons behind them.
rubric.1: I can identify these norms only with a word bank or guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can analyze the norms of language use and visual access, including the history and values of language pride behind them.
rubric.3: I can explain how the norms of language use and visual access grew out of the visual nature of the community.
rubric.2: I can identify the core norms and basic reasons — but explaining how they grew out of the community’s visual nature isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can connect these norms to the community’s visual nature only with guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can connect language-use and access norms to language pride and audism debates — analyzing a real situation (a school, a workplace) and proposing what cultural competence would look like.
rubric.3: I can analyze the norms of language use and visual access, including the history and values of language pride behind them.
rubric.2: I can explain how these norms grew from the community’s visual nature — but analyzing the history and values of language pride behind them isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze these norms only with guiding questions and sources picked for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify these norms even with a word bank and guiding questions.

standards.name: Deaf Media
standards.description: Students know the landscape of Deaf media and engage with Deaf-created works.

targets.statement: I can describe the landscape of Deaf media.

rubric.4: I can describe the major genres of Deaf media — ABC stories, classifier stories, ASL poetry — and what makes each distinctive.
rubric.3: I can identify the major forms of Deaf media (ASL storytelling and literature, Deaf film and theater, Deaf creators online), naming examples of each.
rubric.2: I can name some forms of Deaf media, but I can’t give examples of each.
rubric.1: I can identify forms of Deaf media only with a word bank or examples in front of me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify forms of Deaf media even with a word bank.

rubric.4: I can analyze how Deaf media expresses Deaf experience, identity, and culture across its forms.
rubric.3: I can describe the major genres of Deaf media — ABC stories, classifier stories, ASL poetry — and what makes each distinctive.
rubric.2: I can identify the major forms of Deaf media with examples — but describing the genres and what makes each distinctive isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can describe genres only with notes or guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify forms of Deaf media even with a word bank.

rubric.4: I can curate and critique — situating works in the tradition, comparing how different forms carry Deaf identity, and arguing what a piece contributes to the culture.
rubric.3: I can analyze how Deaf media expresses Deaf experience, identity, and culture across its forms.
rubric.2: I can describe the genres of Deaf media and what makes each distinctive — but analyzing how the forms express Deaf experience and identity isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze Deaf media only with guiding questions and selected examples.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify forms of Deaf media even with a word bank.

targets.statement: I can engage with Deaf-created works.

rubric.4: I can summarize and respond to Deaf-created works, pointing out the ASL features and cultural content within them.
rubric.3: I can watch accessible Deaf-created works and identify the basic story or message.
rubric.2: I can watch a Deaf-created work, but I come away unsure what happened or what the message was.
rubric.1: I can identify the story of a work only with a second viewing, guiding questions, or a partner.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify a work’s basic story even with guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can analyze Deaf-created works — connecting the techniques a creator uses and the themes they explore to Deaf culture — and compare works or creators.
rubric.3: I can summarize and respond to Deaf-created works, pointing out the ASL features and cultural content within them.
rubric.2: I can identify the basic story or message of a Deaf-created work — but summarizing it and pointing out its ASL features and cultural content isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can point out features in a work only with prompts or a guided rewatch.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify a work’s basic story even with guiding questions.

rubric.4: I can produce original critical analysis — close-reading a work’s technique, connecting it across creators and eras, and presenting my interpretation in ASL.
rubric.3: I can analyze Deaf-created works — connecting the techniques a creator uses (classifier work, cinematographic signing) and the themes they explore to Deaf culture — and I can compare works or creators.
rubric.2: I can summarize works and point out ASL features and cultural content — but connecting techniques and themes to Deaf culture, and comparing works or creators, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze a work only with guiding questions and a guided rewatch.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t identify a work’s basic story even with guiding questions.

standards.name: Language & Cultural Comparisons
standards.description: Students compare ASL and Deaf culture with English and hearing culture, including the effects of language contact.

targets.statement: I can compare ASL and English as languages.

rubric.4: I can describe structural differences between ASL and English and how each language expresses the same ideas differently.
rubric.3: I can identify basic differences between ASL and English, and explain that ASL is a distinct language with its own grammar — not signed English.
rubric.2: I can state that ASL and English are different, but I can’t name the basic differences or explain why ASL isn’t signed English.
rubric.1: I can identify differences only with examples laid out and guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t name a difference between ASL and English even with examples.

rubric.4: I can analyze what the comparison between ASL and English reveals about language itself, including common misconceptions about signed languages.
rubric.3: I can describe structural differences between ASL and English and how each language expresses the same ideas in different ways.
rubric.2: I can identify basic differences and explain that ASL has its own grammar — but describing structural differences, and how each language expresses the same idea, isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can describe structural differences only with examples and guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t name a difference between ASL and English even with examples.

rubric.4: I can teach this comparison — correcting misconceptions in real conversations with evidence, and extending the analysis to other signed languages and what they show about language itself.
rubric.3: I can analyze what the comparison between ASL and English reveals about language itself, including common misconceptions about signed languages.
rubric.2: I can describe the structural differences between the languages — but analyzing what the comparison reveals about language, and addressing misconceptions, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze the comparison only with guiding questions and prepared examples.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t name a difference between ASL and English even with examples.

targets.statement: I can explain how language contact has shaped ASL.

rubric.4: I can analyze the effects of English contact on ASL, including where English influence is debated within the Deaf community.
rubric.3: I can describe ways English has influenced ASL through language contact, such as fingerspelling, initialized signs, and mouthing.
rubric.2: I can name one or two ways English shows up in ASL when examples are in front of me, but I can’t describe the influence on my own.
rubric.1: I can describe language contact only with a word bank or guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t describe English influence on ASL even with examples.

rubric.4: I can weigh in on the debates themselves — initialized signs, signed systems, English-influenced variation — representing multiple Deaf community positions fairly and defending my own.
rubric.3: I can analyze the effects of English contact on ASL, including where English influence is debated within the Deaf community.
rubric.2: I can describe how English has influenced ASL through contact — but analyzing the effects, and where the influence is debated in the community, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze contact effects only with sources and guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t describe English influence on ASL even with examples.

targets.statement: I can compare Deaf and hearing cultural practices.

rubric.4: I can compare the values underneath Deaf and hearing cultural practices, including practices from my own cultures.
rubric.3: I can identify basic differences between Deaf and hearing cultural practices.
rubric.2: I can name a Deaf cultural practice or two, but I can’t lay out how they differ from hearing practices.
rubric.1: I can identify differences in practices only with examples and guiding questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t name a difference between Deaf and hearing practices even with examples.

rubric.4: I can analyze Deaf and hearing cultural perspectives, including how hearing assumptions look from a Deaf point of view.
rubric.3: I can compare the values underneath Deaf and hearing cultural practices, including practices from my own cultures.
rubric.2: I can identify basic differences between Deaf and hearing practices — but comparing the values underneath them isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can compare cultural values only with guiding questions or sentence frames.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t name a difference between Deaf and hearing practices even with examples.

rubric.4: I can flip frames at will — analyzing a real situation from a Deaf perspective, naming the hearing assumptions in play, and articulating what each culture’s values would change about it.
rubric.3: I can analyze Deaf and hearing cultural perspectives, including how hearing assumptions look from a Deaf point of view.
rubric.2: I can compare the values underneath Deaf and hearing practices — but analyzing whole perspectives, including how hearing assumptions look from the Deaf side, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can analyze cultural perspectives only with guiding questions and examples.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t name a difference between Deaf and hearing practices even with examples.

standards.name: 5 Parameters
standards.description: Students produce signs with accurate parameters, acquire new signs precisely, and use parameter knowledge to modify and combine signs.

targets.statement: I can produce signs with correct parameters.

rubric.4: I can sign familiar signs with correct parameters consistently — including the facial and non-manual parts of signs — and keep my parameters accurate through whole phrases and several signs in a row.
rubric.3: I can sign familiar signs with all five parameters correct most of the time. I make occasional parameter errors, and I can fix them when they’re pointed out.
rubric.2: I can sign familiar signs that others recognize, but I make frequent errors in one or more parameters (handshape, movement, location, palm orientation, or expression) and need reminders to catch them.
rubric.1: I can sign familiar signs with correct parameters only when someone models the sign for me or coaches me through it.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signs aren’t recognizable even with a model and help.

rubric.4: I can sign with every parameter correct consistently, keeping my accuracy through connected, unscripted signing.
rubric.3: I can sign familiar signs with correct parameters consistently — including the non-manual parts — and keep my parameters accurate through phrases and several signs in a row.
rubric.2: I can sign familiar signs with parameters correct most of the time, fixing occasional errors when they’re pointed out — but my accuracy doesn’t yet hold up across full phrases, and non-manual parts get dropped.
rubric.1: I can sign with correct parameters only with a model to copy or step-by-step corrections; on my own, my parameter errors are frequent.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signs aren’t recognizable even with a model and help.

rubric.4: I can keep every parameter accurate even in fast, complex, or unplanned signing, and I use parameters deliberately — adjusting a sign on purpose to shade its meaning, and catching and fixing my own slips immediately without losing flow.
rubric.3: I can sign with every parameter correct consistently, keeping my accuracy through connected, unscripted signing.
rubric.2: I can sign with correct parameters through phrases and strings of familiar signs, but my accuracy slips when the signing gets longer or unscripted.
rubric.1: I can hold parameter accuracy only with support such as a model, prompts, or repeated corrections; on my own, errors show up regularly even in familiar signing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signs aren’t recognizable even with a model and help.

targets.statement: I can pick up new signs and use parameter patterns to build on them.

rubric.4: I can pick up new signs from a model with mostly correct parameters, modify familiar signs within their sign families in guided practice, and start adjusting signs when I combine them.
rubric.3: I can learn a new sign by copying a model, capturing most of its parameters on the first try.
rubric.2: I can copy a new sign from a model, but I usually miss more than one parameter and need several tries before the sign is recognizable.
rubric.1: I can copy a new sign only when it’s signed slowly, several times, with someone coaching me through each parameter.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a new sign even with a slow model and coaching.

rubric.4: I can pick up new signs precisely from a model, use sign families and parameter patterns to make educated attempts at signs I haven’t been taught and to modify signs for new meaning, and blend combined signs smoothly.
rubric.3: I can pick up new signs from a model with mostly correct parameters, modify familiar signs within their sign families in guided practice, and begin adjusting signs when I combine them.
rubric.2: I can learn a new sign by copying a model, capturing most of its parameters — but modifying signs within sign families and adjusting signs in combinations isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can pick up or modify signs only with a model to copy and step-by-step corrections; on my own, my versions miss parameters.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a new sign even with a slow model and coaching.

rubric.4: I can acquire a new sign accurately from a single viewing, and my educated attempts at untaught signs are usually close enough that a fluent signer reads them without help — I build and modify signs the way the language itself does.
rubric.3: I can pick up new signs precisely from a model, use sign families and parameter patterns to make educated attempts at signs I haven’t been taught and to modify signs for new meaning, and blend combined signs smoothly.
rubric.2: I can pick up new signs from a model with mostly correct parameters and modify familiar signs in guided practice — but my attempts at untaught signs and my blends of combined signs aren’t reliable yet.
rubric.1: I can attempt new or modified signs only with a model and coaching; without support, my attempts miss key parameters.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a new sign even with a slow model and coaching.

standards.name: Vocabulary
standards.description: Students build and use a cumulative sign vocabulary: retaining signs over time, signing concepts rather than English words, and covering gaps with the vocabulary they own.

targets.statement: I can use and keep building a sign vocabulary.

rubric.4: I can use an expanding vocabulary across taught topics, keeping signs from earlier units and using them in familiar conversations.
rubric.3: I can use core everyday vocabulary and a small set of well-practiced signs from taught topics in familiar, practiced contexts.
rubric.2: I can use some everyday vocabulary in practiced contexts, but I lose signs soon after a unit ends and often need a prompt to retrieve them.
rubric.1: I can produce taught vocabulary only with prompts, a word bank, or a model to copy.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t produce taught signs even with prompts and a model.

rubric.4: I can use a broad vocabulary across familiar and many less familiar topics, retaining signs from across my years of study.
rubric.3: I can use an expanding vocabulary across taught topics, retaining signs from earlier units and applying them in familiar conversations.
rubric.2: I can use core everyday vocabulary and well-practiced signs in familiar, practiced contexts — but signs from earlier units fade, and my vocabulary doesn’t yet hold up in real conversations.
rubric.1: I can use taught vocabulary only with heavy prompting or a review right beforehand; on my own, retrieval fails often.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t produce taught signs even with prompts and a model.

rubric.4: I can pull the right sign quickly on nearly any topic, and my vocabulary keeps growing on its own — I pick up signs from Deaf signers and media and put them to use without being taught them in class.
rubric.3: I can use a broad vocabulary across familiar and many less familiar topics, retaining signs from across my years of study.
rubric.2: I can use a solid vocabulary across taught topics and keep signs from earlier units — but on less familiar topics, my vocabulary thins out quickly.
rubric.1: I can use my vocabulary only with prompts or a pre-review; without support, I fall back on the same small set of signs.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t produce taught signs even with prompts and a model.

targets.statement: I can sign concepts instead of English words.

rubric.4: I can sign concepts rather than English words for taught multi-meaning words, even though my errors are still common.
rubric.3: I can sign the concept rather than the English word on a few explicitly taught examples (such as the different signs for ’run’).
rubric.2: I can match signs to English words, but I mostly sign word-for-word; the conceptually right sign shows up only when someone reminds me.
rubric.1: I can choose the conceptually correct sign only when someone walks me through what the sentence actually means.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I sign English word-for-word even with coaching.

rubric.4: I can sign concepts rather than English words for most multi-meaning words, choosing the correct sign for each sense, with occasional errors on unfamiliar ones.
rubric.3: I can sign concepts rather than English words for taught multi-meaning words, though my errors are still common.
rubric.2: I can sign the concept instead of the English word on a few explicitly taught examples — but beyond those, word-for-word signing takes over.
rubric.1: I can pick the conceptually correct sign only with reminders or a walk-through of the meaning.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I sign English word-for-word even with coaching.

rubric.4: I can choose conceptually accurate signs automatically, even for words and idioms I’ve never seen signed — meaning drives my signing, not English glosses.
rubric.3: I can sign the concept rather than the English word for most multi-meaning words, choosing the correct sign for each sense, with occasional errors on unfamiliar ones.
rubric.2: I can sign concepts for taught multi-meaning words — but my errors are still common, and unfamiliar multi-meaning words usually come out as the wrong sign.
rubric.1: I can keep conceptual accuracy only with reminders; on my own, English glosses drive my sign choices.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I sign English word-for-word even with coaching.

targets.statement: I can cover vocabulary gaps with the signs I own.

rubric.4: I can choose between taught near-synonyms, and I can work around vocabulary gaps using simpler signs I already know.
rubric.3: I can cover a vocabulary gap with a sign I know, a gesture, or fingerspelling instead of stopping.
rubric.2: I can sometimes keep going past a missing sign, but I often freeze or drop into English when I don’t know a sign.
rubric.1: I can get past a missing sign only when someone prompts me with a strategy (fingerspell it, gesture, point).
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I stop completely at a missing sign even with prompting.

rubric.4: I can choose among near-synonyms for meaning and context, and I can express concepts that don’t have a one-to-one sign by combining vocabulary I own.
rubric.3: I can choose between taught near-synonyms, and I can work around vocabulary gaps using simpler signs I already know.
rubric.2: I can cover a missing sign with a known sign, a gesture, or fingerspelling — but my workarounds are clunky, and choosing between near-synonyms isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can work around a gap only when someone prompts the strategy; otherwise I stall or switch to English.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I stop completely at a missing sign even with prompting.

rubric.4: I can paraphrase around any gap so smoothly that the viewer rarely notices a sign was missing, and my sign choices read like a skilled signer’s — precise, contextual, and varied.
rubric.3: I can choose among near-synonyms for meaning and context, and I can express untaught concepts by combining vocabulary I own.
rubric.2: I can choose between taught near-synonyms and work around gaps with simpler signs — but expressing untaught concepts by combining my vocabulary is still hit-or-miss.
rubric.1: I can express a concept I lack a sign for only with prompting and time to plan it out.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I stop completely at a missing sign even with prompting.

standards.name: ASL Grammar
standards.description: Students structure their signing with ASL syntax: building sentences with ASL conventions, connecting ideas into complex sentences, and restructuring ideas visually rather than translating from English.

targets.statement: I can build sentences with ASL structure.

rubric.4: I can structure my own sentences on familiar topics using ASL conventions — topic-comment, time-first ordering, and question and negation placement — with developing consistency.
rubric.3: I can produce short sentences using taught ASL patterns, including time-first ordering and WH-questions at the end, in practiced contexts.
rubric.2: I can produce a taught pattern when the frame is set up for me, but English order takes over once I leave the practiced frame.
rubric.1: I can build an ASL-ordered sentence only with a model or a frame to fill in.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my sentences come out in English order even with a model.

rubric.4: I can structure sentences using ASL conventions — topic-comment, time-first ordering, and placement of questions and negation — consistently in unscripted signing.
rubric.3: I can structure my own sentences on familiar topics using ASL conventions — topic-comment, time-first ordering, and question and negation placement — with developing consistency.
rubric.2: I can produce taught patterns such as time-first and WH-final in practiced contexts — but in my own sentences, ASL structure comes and goes and English order creeps back.
rubric.1: I can use ASL sentence structure only with frames, models, or corrections as I go.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my sentences come out in English order even with a model.

rubric.4: I can keep ASL structure intact in fast, complex, unplanned signing, and I bend structures on purpose — fronting a topic for emphasis or reshaping a sentence mid-stream — without losing grammatical control.
rubric.3: I can structure sentences using ASL conventions — topic-comment structure, time-first ordering, and placement of questions and negation — consistently in unscripted signing.
rubric.2: I can structure sentences with ASL conventions on familiar topics — but consistency isn’t there yet in unscripted signing, where English order resurfaces.
rubric.1: I can hold ASL structure only with prompts or planning time; unscripted, my sentences default to English order.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my sentences come out in English order even with a model.

targets.statement: I can connect ideas into complex sentences.

rubric.4: I can connect ideas into complex sentences, sequencing clauses the way ASL conventions call for, including conditionals, cause and effect, and comparisons.
rubric.3: I can connect two ideas into one sentence using taught structures such as conditionals and cause and effect.
rubric.2: I can sign two related ideas as separate sentences, but joining them into one structure only works with a frame or model in front of me.
rubric.1: I can connect two ideas only when the structure is modeled and I’m walked through it step by step.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t join two ideas even with a modeled frame.

rubric.4: I can build multi-clause sentences fluidly in unscripted signing — layering conditionals, comparisons, and cause-and-effect chains — and I choose the clause order that makes my point land best.
rubric.3: I can connect ideas into complex sentences, sequencing clauses according to ASL conventions, including conditionals, cause and effect, and comparisons.
rubric.2: I can connect two ideas with taught structures such as conditionals and cause and effect — but longer chains of clauses and comparisons still break apart or slide into English order.
rubric.1: I can build a complex sentence only with planning time or a structure laid out for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t join two ideas even with a modeled frame.

targets.statement: I can restructure ideas visually instead of translating from English.

rubric.4: I can restructure taught sentence types visually, such as comparisons and descriptions, setting up elements in space within practiced structures.
rubric.3: I can restructure one taught sentence type away from English order — usually moving the question sign to the end.
rubric.2: I can repeat a restructured sentence after a model, but on my own I build it in English order first and have to fix it.
rubric.1: I can restructure a sentence only when someone shows me the ASL version to copy.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t move a sentence out of English order even with a model.

rubric.4: I can restructure ideas visually rather than translating from English — setting up elements in space and commenting on them — in practiced structures and increasingly in my own signing.
rubric.3: I can restructure taught sentence types visually, such as comparisons and descriptions, setting up elements in space within practiced structures.
rubric.2: I can restructure one taught sentence type away from English order — but visual restructuring like comparisons and spatial setups isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can set up a visual structure only when it’s modeled first and I’m coached through it.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t move a sentence out of English order even with a model.

rubric.4: I can think visually first: even on new topics, my signing organizes ideas spatially by default, with no English sentence structure left to undo.
rubric.3: I can restructure ideas visually rather than translating from English, setting up elements in space and commenting on them, in practiced structures and increasingly in my own signing.
rubric.2: I can restructure taught sentence types visually in practiced structures — but in my own signing, I still translate from English more often than I build visually.
rubric.1: I can build a visual structure only with a model or step-by-step coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t move a sentence out of English order even with a model.

standards.name: Clarity-Pacing-Fluency
standards.description: Students sign watchably: forming signs clearly and delivering them with steady, connected flow.

targets.statement: I can form my signs clearly and visibly.

rubric.4: I can produce clear, fully formed signs within the signing space in practiced performances and on familiar prompts, not just fully rehearsed pieces.
rubric.3: I can sign deliberately and visibly in prepared, rehearsed performances, forming each sign fully and keeping it inside the signing space, even if I’m slow.
rubric.2: I can get through a rehearsed performance, but my signs shrink, drift out of the signing space, or blur together in stretches.
rubric.1: I can form signs clearly only when someone models them or slows me down sign by sign.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signing isn’t readable even with a model and coaching.

rubric.4: I can produce clear, fully formed signs within the signing space, including when I’m signing on a topic with no preparation.
rubric.3: I can produce clear, fully formed signs within the signing space in practiced performances and on familiar prompts.
rubric.2: I can form signs fully in rehearsed performances — but on familiar prompts without rehearsal, my clarity drops and signs start to shrink or blur.
rubric.1: I can keep my production clear only with a model, a slow pace set for me, or constant reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signing isn’t readable even with a model and coaching.

rubric.4: My production stays crisp no matter the situation — speed, nerves, or hard content don’t degrade it — and viewers never have to work to read my signs.
rubric.3: I can produce clear, fully formed signs within the signing space, including when I’m signing on an unprepared topic.
rubric.2: I can keep my signs clear and fully formed in practiced work and on familiar prompts — but put on the spot, my clarity slips.
rubric.1: I can keep production clear only in short, supported stretches with prompts to reset.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signing isn’t readable even with a model and coaching.

targets.statement: I can deliver my signing at a steady, connected pace.

rubric.4: I can deliver practiced and familiar signing at a steady pace with developing phrasing, with hesitations falling between ideas more often than between every sign.
rubric.3: I can deliver rehearsed material with an even rhythm from start to finish — not racing through memorized strings, not freezing between signs.
rubric.2: I can get through rehearsed material, but my delivery lurches: racing through the parts I know and stalling between signs.
rubric.1: I can keep a rhythm only when someone signs along with me or sets the pace for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t get through rehearsed material even with support.

rubric.4: I can deliver impromptu signing at a steady pace with connected phrasing and only occasional false starts, and I’m beginning to vary my speed for emphasis in prepared work.
rubric.3: I can deliver practiced and familiar signing at a steady pace with developing phrasing — my hesitations fall between ideas more often than between every sign.
rubric.2: I can deliver rehearsed material with an even rhythm — but on familiar prompts without rehearsal, my pacing breaks down and I hesitate between every sign.
rubric.1: I can hold a steady pace only with a practice run right before or someone pacing me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t get through rehearsed material even with support.

rubric.4: My pacing reads like a fluent signer’s: impromptu signing flows without visible effort, and I use speed deliberately — slowing down, snapping a sign, holding a pause — to shape meaning.
rubric.3: I can deliver impromptu signing at a steady pace with connected phrasing and only occasional false starts, and I’m beginning to vary my speed for emphasis in prepared work.
rubric.2: I can deliver practiced and familiar signing at a steady, phrased pace — but impromptu signing still stalls and fragments when I have to compose on the spot.
rubric.1: I can keep impromptu signing moving only with prompts, restarts, and patience from my viewer.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t get through rehearsed material even with support.

standards.name: Numbers, Counting & Fingerspelling
standards.description: Students produce fingerspelling, lexicalized fingerspelling, and ASL number systems accurately and smoothly within their signing.

targets.statement: I can fingerspell clearly and smoothly.

rubric.4: I can fingerspell familiar words with an even rhythm and growing speed, and spell new words without disrupting the flow of my signing.
rubric.3: I can fingerspell practiced, familiar words with correct letter formation, minimal bouncing, and a slow, careful pace — and I use fingerspelling when a word calls for it instead of avoiding it.
rubric.2: I can fingerspell short practiced words, but my letters slip, my hand bounces, and I avoid fingerspelling when I can.
rubric.1: I can fingerspell only letter by letter, with a model or an alphabet chart to follow.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t form the manual alphabet even with a model.

rubric.4: I can fingerspell most words where it’s appropriate, including names and unfamiliar terms, smoothly and without interrupting my pacing.
rubric.3: I can fingerspell familiar words with an even rhythm and growing speed, and I can spell new words without disrupting the flow of my signing.
rubric.2: I can fingerspell practiced words with correct formation at a slow, careful pace — but my rhythm is uneven, and spelling a new word stops my signing.
rubric.1: I can fingerspell smoothly only when I rehearse the word first or someone sets a slow pace with me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t form the manual alphabet even with a model.

rubric.4: My fingerspelling approaches native speed and shape: words come out as smooth units with natural rhythm, and I’m comfortable spelling anything, anywhere in my signing.
rubric.3: I can fingerspell most words where it’s appropriate — names, unfamiliar terms — smoothly and without interrupting my pacing.
rubric.2: I can fingerspell familiar words with even rhythm and fold new words into my signing — but unfamiliar terms and names still slow me down or break my pacing.
rubric.1: I can fingerspell unfamiliar words only slowly and deliberately, with rehearsal or support.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t form the manual alphabet even with a model.

targets.statement: I can produce lexicalized fingerspelling as fused signs.

rubric.4: I can produce common taught lexicalized fingerspelling as fused units within practiced signing.
rubric.3: I can produce a small set of practiced lexicalized signs (such as #BUS) as taught units.
rubric.2: I can spell the letters of taught lexicalized signs, but they come out as plain fingerspelling rather than the fused sign.
rubric.1: I can produce a lexicalized sign only right after a model shows it to me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t produce a lexicalized sign even right after a model.

rubric.4: I can produce common lexicalized fingerspelling as single fused units inside my own signing.
rubric.3: I can produce common taught lexicalized fingerspelling as fused units within practiced signing.
rubric.2: I can produce a small set of lexicalized signs as taught units — but inside connected signing they fall apart into letter-by-letter spelling.
rubric.1: I can produce lexicalized signs only in isolation with a model to copy.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t produce a lexicalized sign even right after a model.

rubric.4: I can use a wide range of lexicalized forms naturally and pick up new ones from Deaf signers, fusing them the way the community signs them.
rubric.3: I can produce common lexicalized fingerspelling as single fused units inside my own signing.
rubric.2: I can produce common lexicalized fingerspelling as fused units in practiced signing — but in my own spontaneous signing they still surface as plain fingerspelling.
rubric.1: I can produce lexicalized forms only in drilled phrases with support.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t produce a lexicalized sign even right after a model.

targets.statement: I can produce numbers across ASL number systems.

rubric.4: I can produce numbers across additional taught systems, such as age, money, and time, choosing the right system in practiced contexts and counting quickly.
rubric.3: I can produce cardinal and ordinal numbers with correct formation, counting comfortably to 100.
rubric.2: I can count and form many numbers, but my formation errors are common past the teens, and ordinals trip me up.
rubric.1: I can produce numbers only by copying a model number by number.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t form numbers even with a model.

rubric.4: I can produce numbers accurately across a variety of systems, choosing the correct system for the context and signing several numbers in a row at a steady pace.
rubric.3: I can produce numbers across additional taught systems, such as age, money, and time, choosing the right system in practiced contexts and counting quickly.
rubric.2: I can produce cardinal and ordinal numbers with correct formation and count comfortably to 100 — but the special systems (age, money, time) mix together, and I default to plain counting.
rubric.1: I can use the number systems only with a chart, prompts, or a model for each one.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t form numbers even with a model.

rubric.4: I can handle any number situation without thinking about it — switching systems mid-sentence, incorporating numbers into signs, and keeping number-heavy signing at full conversational speed.
rubric.3: I can produce numbers accurately across a variety of number systems, choosing the correct system for the context, and I can sign several numbers in a row — dates, phone numbers, identification numbers — at a steady pace.
rubric.2: I can produce numbers across the taught systems in practiced contexts — but choosing the right system on the fly and signing strings of numbers at a steady pace isn’t reliable yet.
rubric.1: I can produce number strings only slowly, with planning or prompting between numbers.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t form numbers even with a model.

standards.name: Initiating and Maintaining Interaction
standards.description: Students carry their share of signed conversations: starting and ending exchanges, asking their own questions, and keeping the conversation moving.

targets.statement: I can start, carry, and end signed conversations.

rubric.4: I can initiate, sustain, and close short conversations on familiar topics, using practiced openings, closings, and small-talk scripts as a base.
rubric.3: I can open and close short exchanges using memorized greetings, farewells, and pleasantries.
rubric.2: I can return a greeting when someone signs it to me, but I rarely start or end an exchange myself.
rubric.1: I can open or close an exchange only with a script in front of me or the teacher feeding me the signs.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t greet or close even with a script.

rubric.4: I can initiate, sustain, and close unscripted conversations on familiar and some less familiar topics, including meeting and getting to know new signers.
rubric.3: I can initiate, sustain, and close short conversations on familiar topics, using practiced openings, closings, and small-talk scripts as a base.
rubric.2: I can open and close exchanges with memorized greetings and pleasantries — but sustaining a real conversation between the opening and the closing isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can hold a conversation only when my partner slows down, feeds me lines, or sticks to the script.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t greet or close even with a script.

rubric.4: I can hold my own in any signed conversation — unfamiliar topics, group settings, native signers at full speed — and the exchange flows for both of us.
rubric.3: I can initiate, sustain, and close unscripted conversations on familiar and some less familiar topics, including meeting and getting to know new signers.
rubric.2: I can initiate, sustain, and close short conversations on familiar topics from a practiced base — but unscripted conversations, and conversations with new signers, still fall apart on me.
rubric.1: I can manage an unscripted conversation only with a patient partner who simplifies and supports.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t greet or close even with a script.

targets.statement: I can ask my own questions in conversation.

rubric.4: I can ask my own questions and follow-ups beyond the script to learn more about a partner or topic.
rubric.3: I can ask and answer common scripted questions about myself and familiar topics within a rehearsed exchange.
rubric.2: I can answer scripted questions when asked, but I don’t ask questions back without prompting.
rubric.1: I can ask or answer questions only with a script or the teacher modeling each one.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t ask or answer even scripted questions with support.

rubric.4: I can pursue my own lines of inquiry in conversation, asking original questions and follow-ups to investigate what I want to know.
rubric.3: I can ask my own questions and follow-ups beyond the script to learn more about a partner or topic.
rubric.2: I can ask and answer common scripted questions in a rehearsed exchange — but my own questions, beyond the script, are rare.
rubric.1: I can come up with a question of my own only with prompting or planning time.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t ask or answer even scripted questions with support.

rubric.4: I drive real conversations with my questions — probing, redirecting, and following curiosity wherever it goes — the way I would in my first language.
rubric.3: I can pursue my own lines of inquiry in conversation, asking original questions and follow-ups to investigate what I want to know about a person or topic.
rubric.2: I can ask my own questions and follow-ups beyond the script — but a sustained line of inquiry, with follow-ups building on answers, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can keep a line of questioning going only with prompts or planned questions.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t ask or answer even scripted questions with support.

targets.statement: I can keep the conversation moving.

rubric.4: I can maintain conversational flow with shorter pauses, recovering from lulls and continuing the exchange rather than stalling out.
rubric.3: I can stay in an exchange for several turns, attempting a response rather than giving up or breaking into English.
rubric.2: I can take a turn or two, but when I don’t know what to say, I drop out of the exchange or switch to English.
rubric.1: I can stay in an exchange only with the teacher prompting me through each turn.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I leave the exchange even with prompting.

rubric.4: I can maintain conversational flow, responding without long pauses or visible searching, and drawing on pleasantries and go-to phrases to bridge lulls.
rubric.3: I can keep a conversation moving with shorter pauses, recovering from lulls and continuing rather than stalling or dropping out.
rubric.2: I can stay in an exchange for several turns and attempt responses — but my pauses are long, and lulls tend to end the conversation.
rubric.1: I can keep a conversation going only with my partner carrying it or prompting me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I leave the exchange even with prompting.

rubric.4: I carry conversations effortlessly — no visible searching, lulls turned into new threads — and I can keep an exchange alive even with a reluctant or struggling partner.
rubric.3: I can keep conversations flowing — responding without long pauses or visible searching, and drawing on a bank of pleasantries and go-to phrases to bridge lulls.
rubric.2: I can keep a conversation moving and recover from lulls — but long pauses and visible searching still show up, especially on less familiar topics.
rubric.1: I can keep flow only when my partner manages the rhythm of the conversation for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I leave the exchange even with prompting.

standards.name: Clarification and Repair
standards.description: Students keep conversations on track when communication breaks down, signaling and requesting help as receivers, repairing their own message as senders, and monitoring and correcting themselves as they sign.

targets.statement: I can signal when I don’t understand and ask for the help I need.

rubric.4: I can back-channel at some points during exchanges to show I’m following, and signal non-understanding using practiced requests — asking for repetition, slowing, or fingerspelling.
rubric.3: I can signal when I don’t understand and ask for help using basic practiced signs such as AGAIN or SLOW.
rubric.2: When I’m lost, I tend to freeze, nod along, or wait it out — I signal non-understanding only when prompted.
rubric.1: I can signal non-understanding only when the teacher prompts me to use AGAIN or SLOW.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I let breakdowns pass without signaling even when prompted.

rubric.4: I can back-channel appropriately so my partner knows the message is landing, signal promptly when it isn’t, and request the specific kind of help I need.
rubric.3: I can back-channel at some points during exchanges to show I’m following, and I can signal non-understanding using practiced requests — asking for repetition, slowing, or fingerspelling.
rubric.2: I can signal non-understanding with basic signs like AGAIN or SLOW — but back-channeling and choosing the right kind of request aren’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can use clarification requests only when prompted in the moment.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I let breakdowns pass without signaling even when prompted.

rubric.4: My feedback loop is constant and unobtrusive — partners always know what’s landing, and my repair requests are so specific and well-timed that breakdowns barely register.
rubric.3: I can back-channel appropriately so my partner knows the message is landing, signal promptly when it isn’t, and request the kind of help I need — repetition, slowing, rephrasing, or fingerspelling.
rubric.2: I can back-channel and signal non-understanding with practiced requests — but my signals come late, and I default to asking for repetition instead of the specific help I need.
rubric.1: I can manage the feedback loop only with reminders to back-channel and signal.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I let breakdowns pass without signaling even when prompted.

targets.statement: I can repair my message when my partner doesn’t understand.

rubric.4: I can attempt at least one repair beyond repetition when a partner doesn’t understand me — trying a different sign, fingerspelling, or simplifying.
rubric.3: I can respond to a partner’s confusion by repeating my message slowly and carefully.
rubric.2: When my partner looks confused, I tend to give up or switch to English instead of repeating my message.
rubric.1: I can repair my message only when the teacher tells me to repeat it and how.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I abandon my message at the first breakdown even with support.

rubric.4: I can repair my own message until it lands — rephrasing with different signs, restructuring, fingerspelling, or depicting.
rubric.3: I can attempt at least one repair beyond simple repetition when a partner doesn’t understand — trying a different sign, fingerspelling, or simplifying.
rubric.2: I can repeat my message slowly and carefully when my partner is confused — but repetition is my only repair.
rubric.1: I can try a different repair only when someone suggests it.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I abandon my message at the first breakdown even with support.

rubric.4: I diagnose why a message failed and pick the fix that works on the first try — rebuilding an idea two or three different ways without breaking conversational rhythm.
rubric.3: I can repair my own message when a partner doesn’t understand — rephrasing with different signs, restructuring, fingerspelling, or depicting until it lands.
rubric.2: I can attempt a repair beyond repetition — but if my first alternate attempt fails, I run out of strategies.
rubric.1: I can work through a failed message only with coaching on what to try.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I abandon my message at the first breakdown even with support.

targets.statement: I can catch and correct my own errors as I sign.

rubric.4: I can catch my own errors and correct them, even if the correction interrupts the flow of the exchange.
rubric.3: I can catch my own errors as I sign — especially in fingerspelling — even if fixing them is awkward or stops the exchange.
rubric.2: I keep signing past my errors without noticing them; I fix them only when someone points them out.
rubric.1: I can catch an error only when someone flags that I made one.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I don’t catch my errors even when they’re flagged.

rubric.4: I can monitor as I sign — checking that my partner is following and correcting my own errors smoothly, without halting the conversation.
rubric.3: I can catch my own errors and correct them, even if the correction interrupts the flow of the exchange.
rubric.2: I can catch some of my own errors, especially in fingerspelling — but many slip by, and my corrections stop the exchange.
rubric.1: I can catch and fix errors only when they’re pointed out.
