rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I don’t catch my errors even when they’re flagged.

rubric.4: My self-monitoring is silent and total — errors get fixed in-stride before most viewers notice, and I adjust my signing in real time to my partner’s comprehension.
rubric.3: I can monitor the exchange as I sign — checking that my partner is following and correcting my own errors smoothly, without halting the conversation.
rubric.2: I can catch and correct my own errors — but the corrections still interrupt my flow, and I lose track of whether my partner is following.
rubric.1: I can monitor and self-correct only with prompts or a slowed pace.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I don’t catch my errors even when they’re flagged.

standards.name: Communication Norms
standards.description: Students follow Deaf cultural and visual communication norms: getting attention, managing eye gaze and the visual environment, taking turns, and operating in ASL-only environments.

targets.statement: I can get attention the culturally Deaf way.

rubric.4: I can get attention smoothly without being reminded — choosing politely between a tap and a wave for the situation — and I respond right away when someone seeks mine.
rubric.3: I can get attention with a tap or a wave, and I respond when someone seeks my attention.
rubric.2: I can respond when someone gets my attention, but I fall back on voice or hovering instead of tapping or waving to get theirs.
rubric.1: I can use a tap or wave only when reminded in the moment.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I use voice and English habits even with reminders.

rubric.4: I can get attention using methods suited to the situation — including relaying through another person — and respond appropriately when others seek my attention.
rubric.3: I can get attention using practiced methods such as tapping or waving, and I respond when others seek my attention — though I tend to use the same method no matter the situation.
rubric.2: I can get attention with a tap or wave when I think of it — but I need reminders, and my method or timing is often awkward for the situation.
rubric.1: I can use attention-getting norms only with reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I use voice and English habits even with reminders.

rubric.4: My attention-getting reads as culturally native — taps, waves, light flashes, relays, and judgment calls about urgency and politeness, all matched to the setting without thought.
rubric.3: I can get attention using methods suited to the situation — including relaying through another person when someone is out of reach — and respond appropriately when others seek my attention.
rubric.2: I can get attention with practiced methods and respond when others seek mine — but I use the same method regardless of situation, and relaying through others isn’t part of my toolkit yet.
rubric.1: I can match my method to the situation only with coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I use voice and English habits even with reminders.

targets.statement: I can manage eye gaze and the visual environment.

rubric.4: I can hold visual attention through whole exchanges without reminders, and I position myself with sightlines in mind.
rubric.3: I can keep my eyes on the signer during exchanges — even when I don’t catch everything — and avoid blocking sightlines or walking between signed conversations.
rubric.2: I can watch the signer when reminded, but my eyes drop or wander when I’m lost, and I block sightlines without noticing.
rubric.1: I can keep visual attention only with frequent reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I look away and block signing space even with reminders.

rubric.4: I can manage my eye gaze with cultural awareness — knowing when to look and when looking away is expected — and keep sightlines open as I position and move through signed spaces.
rubric.3: I can maintain eye contact and visual attention during exchanges with only occasional lapses, and I follow positioning and sightline norms with occasional reminders.
rubric.2: I can keep my eyes on the signer through most of an exchange — but my gaze drops when the language gets hard, and positioning norms still need regular reminders.
rubric.1: I can maintain visual attention and positioning only with frequent reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I look away and block signing space even with reminders.

rubric.4: My visual presence is native-like — gaze, positioning, and movement through signed spaces all read as someone raised in the norms, even in crowded or unfamiliar settings.
rubric.3: I can manage my eye gaze with cultural awareness — looking and looking away at the moments the culture expects — and I keep sightlines open as I position and move through signed spaces.
rubric.2: I can maintain eye contact with only occasional lapses and follow positioning norms — but the judgment calls (when looking away is expected, how to move through signed space) aren’t automatic yet.
rubric.1: I can manage gaze and positioning judgment only with coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I look away and block signing space even with reminders.

targets.statement: I can take turns the way signed conversation expects.

rubric.4: I can read when the floor is open and take my turn without it being clearly handed to me, in pairs and small groups.
rubric.3: I can take my turn when it’s clearly given, and I wait while my partner is signing.
rubric.2: I can wait for my turn when reminded, but I start signing over my partner or miss my turn when no one cues me.
rubric.1: I can manage turns only with the teacher directing who signs when.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I sign over others even with direction.

rubric.4: I can manage turn-taking smoothly — taking the floor at natural moments, yielding it appropriately, and judging when and how to interrupt.
rubric.3: I can take my turn when the floor is offered and hold back from signing over my partner, though my timing may be hesitant or occasionally overlap.
rubric.2: I can take clearly given turns and wait while my partner signs — but subtler floor offers get past me, and overlaps still happen without prompting.
rubric.1: I can time my turns only with prompting.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I sign over others even with direction.

rubric.4: My turn-taking is invisible — I read group rhythm, hold or pass the floor with native-like timing, and my interruptions land as natural rather than rude.
rubric.3: I can manage turn-taking smoothly — taking the floor at natural moments, yielding it appropriately, and judging when and how to interrupt.
rubric.2: I can take offered turns and avoid signing over partners — but reading natural moments to take the floor, and judging when an interruption is appropriate, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can manage the rhythm of group turn-taking only with support.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I sign over others even with direction.

targets.statement: I can operate voice-off in signing environments.

rubric.4: I can operate voice-off through extended class activities, handling my conversations and classwork in ASL with only occasional fallback to writing or English.
rubric.3: I can operate voice-off for a class period, following directions and handling routine classroom business in ASL and gesture.
rubric.2: I can stay voice-off for stretches of class, but I break into spoken English when things get confusing or social.
rubric.1: I can stay voice-off only with constant reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t stay voice-off even with reminders.

rubric.4: I can switch to ASL automatically in signing environments and sustain it for events lasting an hour or more, without writing, voicing, or English side conversations.
rubric.3: I can operate voice-off through extended class activities, handling my conversations and classwork in ASL with only occasional fallback to writing or English.
rubric.2: I can stay voice-off for a class period and handle routine business in ASL — but during extended activities, I still fall back on writing or English regularly.
rubric.1: I can sustain voice-off through activities only with frequent reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t stay voice-off even with reminders.

rubric.4: Voice-off isn’t an effort I make — it’s my default in any signing environment, for any duration, including socially, and others never see me reach for English.
rubric.3: I can switch to ASL automatically in signing environments — around Deaf people and at extended events like competitions — and sustain it for an hour or more without writing, voicing, or English side conversations.
rubric.2: I can operate voice-off through extended activities with only occasional fallback — but switching automatically around Deaf people, and lasting through hour-plus events, isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can sustain long voice-off stretches only with check-ins and reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t stay voice-off even with reminders.

standards.name: Grammatical NMM
standards.description: Students produce the facial grammar of ASL: marking sentence types, separating phrases, and keeping non-manual marking alive as they sign.

targets.statement: I can mark sentence types with facial grammar.

rubric.4: I can produce grammatical markers for yes-no questions, WH-questions, and negation reliably, and apply topic and conditional marking in practiced contexts.
rubric.3: I can produce grammatical non-manual markers for yes-no questions (brows up), WH-questions (brows down), and negation (headshake) in short, practiced sentences — even if my timing is rough.
rubric.2: I can produce a marker when I concentrate on it, but my sentences usually go unmarked unless someone cues me.
rubric.1: I can produce a grammatical marker only when cued at the moment I need it.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my face stays unmarked even with cueing.

rubric.4: I can produce the full range of grammatical markers — yes-no, WH, negation, topic, conditional, and rhetorical marking — applying each where appropriate with clean onset and release.
rubric.3: I can produce markers for yes-no questions, WH-questions, and negation reliably, and I can apply topic and conditional marking in practiced contexts.
rubric.2: I can mark yes-no, WH, and negation in short, practiced sentences — but reliability isn’t there yet, and topic and conditional marking only appear with prompting.
rubric.1: I can apply topic or conditional marking only when cued or drilled right before.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my face stays unmarked even with cueing.

rubric.4: My facial grammar is precise enough that someone could identify my sentence types with my hands out of view — and I layer markers (a rhetorical inside a conditional) cleanly when the meaning calls for it.
rubric.3: I can produce the full range of grammatical markers — yes-no, WH, negation, topic, conditional, and rhetorical marking — applying each where appropriate with clean onset and release.
rubric.2: I can mark yes-no, WH, negation, and practiced topic and conditional sentences — but rhetorical marking and clean onset and release across the full range aren’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can produce the full marker range only with cueing or rehearsal.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my face stays unmarked even with cueing.

targets.statement: I can mark the boundaries between sentences.

rubric.4: I can separate phrases and clauses visually, using head movement, pausing, and brow shifts to mark sentence boundaries.
rubric.3: I can mark the boundaries between sentences, using head movement and pausing so one sentence doesn’t run into the next.
rubric.2: My sentences tend to run together; boundaries show up only when I stop and reset.
rubric.1: I can mark a boundary only when cued to pause between sentences.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signing runs together even with cueing.

rubric.4: My boundary marking makes long, complex signing read in clean visual paragraphs — clause edges, asides, and transitions all land without effort from the viewer.
rubric.3: I can separate phrases and clauses visually, using head movement, pausing, and brow shifts to mark sentence boundaries.
rubric.2: I can mark boundaries between sentences with head movement and pausing — but inside long sentences, phrase and clause boundaries still blur.
rubric.1: I can mark phrase boundaries only with coaching or planned pauses.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signing runs together even with cueing.

targets.statement: I can keep non-manual marking alive as I sign.

rubric.4: I can sustain grammatical marking through short, familiar conversations, though markers may drop when my attention shifts.
rubric.3: I can sign with an active face — attempting grammatical marking rather than signing with a blank face — even if my markers are exaggerated or roughly timed.
rubric.2: My face goes blank for stretches when I sign; expression shows up only when someone reminds me.
rubric.1: I can keep my face active only when cued moment to moment.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my face stays blank even with cueing.

rubric.4: I can sustain grammatical marking through extended, unscripted signing, keeping my markers in agreement with my signing without prompting.
rubric.3: I can sustain grammatical non-manual marking through short, familiar conversations, though markers may drop when my attention shifts to vocabulary or structure.
rubric.2: I can sign with an active face and attempt marking — but sustaining it through a conversation isn’t there yet; markers fade after the first sentence or two.
rubric.1: I can keep marking alive only with cues during the conversation.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my face stays blank even with cueing.

rubric.4: Marking never drops, no matter how demanding the content — my facial grammar runs on autopilot while I think about what to say, not how to mark it.
rubric.3: I can sustain grammatical non-manual marking through extended, unscripted signing, keeping my markers in agreement with my signing without prompting.
rubric.2: I can sustain marking through short, familiar conversations — but in extended or unscripted signing, markers still drop when the language gets hard.
rubric.1: I can sustain marking in longer signing only with cueing or planned check-ins.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my face stays blank even with cueing.

standards.name: Head-Face-Body-Eye Gaze
standards.description: Students use face, head, body, and eye gaze expressively: conveying emotion and intensity, portraying characters through role shift, and directing gaze for reference and depiction.

targets.statement: I can convey emotion and intensity with my face and body.

rubric.4: I can convey clear emotions and basic intensity through facial expression and body movement that generally match my message.
rubric.3: I can show basic emotions and other simple expressions while signing, with expression that sometimes matches my message.
rubric.2: I can make expressions on prompted vocabulary, but while signing, my face mostly stays flat.
rubric.1: I can show emotion while signing only when cued to add it.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signing stays flat-faced even with cueing.

rubric.4: I can convey emotion, attitude, and intensity through facial expression, head movement, and body posture that match the content of my message.
rubric.3: I can convey clear emotions and basic intensity through facial expression and body movement that generally match my message.
rubric.2: I can show basic emotions while signing, sometimes matching my message — but the match is inconsistent, and my expression fades during harder stretches.
rubric.1: I can match expression to message only with cues or a model.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signing stays flat-faced even with cueing.

rubric.4: My expressive layer works like an instrument — shading attitude and intensity precisely, dialing emotion up and down mid-sentence — and it never flattens, even in demanding language.
rubric.3: I can convey emotion, attitude, and intensity through facial expression, head movement, and body posture that match the content of my message.
rubric.2: I can convey clear emotions and basic intensity that generally match my message — but attitude, subtler shades, and a full range still flatten when the language gets hard.
rubric.1: I can keep the expressive layer going only with cueing or rehearsal.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my signing stays flat-faced even with cueing.

targets.statement: I can portray characters through role shift.

rubric.4: I can portray two characters in practiced narratives through role shift, keeping each character on a consistent side and using eye gaze and expression to tell them apart.
rubric.3: I can attempt role shift in a short, practiced narrative — assigning characters to a left and right side — even if I show the change of character inconsistently.
rubric.2: I can tell a practiced story’s characters apart in my head, but my body doesn’t show the shift without prompting.
rubric.1: I can shift between characters only when coached through each change.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show a character change even with coaching.

rubric.4: I can portray characters and dialogue through role shift, maintaining each character’s placement across a narrative, adjusting posture and gaze for traits like height or size, and moving cleanly between characters.
rubric.3: I can portray two characters in practiced narratives through role shift, keeping each character on a consistent side and using eye gaze and expression to distinguish them with developing consistency.
rubric.2: I can attempt role shift with characters on a left and right side — but the sides drift, and the character changes don’t read clearly yet.
rubric.1: I can hold two characters apart only with coaching during the story.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show a character change even with coaching.

rubric.4: I can carry an ensemble — three or more distinct characters held across a long narrative, each with their own posture, gaze, and manner — switching cleanly at speed.
rubric.3: I can portray characters and dialogue through role shift — maintaining each character’s placement across a narrative, adjusting my posture and gaze for traits like height or size, and moving cleanly between characters.
rubric.2: I can portray two characters with consistent sides and distinguishing gaze and expression — but holding placement across a whole narrative, and adjusting for character traits, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can maintain characters through a narrative only with coaching or restarts.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t show a character change even with coaching.

targets.statement: I can direct eye gaze for reference and depiction.

rubric.4: I can use eye gaze to locate referents I’ve placed in space, and begin depicting qualities such as distance or size.
rubric.3: I can use eye gaze to look at referents I’ve placed in space within short, practiced sentences.
rubric.2: I can place a referent in space, but my eyes don’t go to it unless someone reminds me.
rubric.1: I can direct my gaze to a referent only when prompted in the moment.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my gaze stays on my hands or the viewer even with prompting.

rubric.4: I can direct my eye gaze deliberately — locating referents in space and depicting their qualities, such as distance, size, or height.
rubric.3: I can use eye gaze to locate referents I’ve placed in space, and I’m beginning to depict qualities such as distance or size with my gaze.
rubric.2: I can look at referents in practiced sentences — but in my own signing, my gaze forgets the referents, and depictive gaze isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can use depictive gaze only when cued.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my gaze stays on my hands or the viewer even with prompting.

rubric.4: My gaze does grammatical and depictive work without my thinking about it — locating, agreeing, depicting, and showing where characters look — woven through every scene I sign.
rubric.3: I can direct my eye gaze deliberately, locating referents in space and depicting their qualities, such as distance, size, or height.
rubric.2: I can use gaze to locate my referents and sometimes depict qualities — but deliberate, consistent gaze work across my signing isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can sustain gaze work only with cueing or planned scenes.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my gaze stays on my hands or the viewer even with prompting.

standards.name: Mouth Morphemes
standards.description: Students use the mouth as a meaningful channel: producing ASL mouth morphemes and keeping mouth activity ASL-driven rather than mouthing English.

targets.statement: I can produce ASL mouth morphemes.

rubric.4: I can produce taught mouth morphemes for size and distance, along with mouth morphemes tied to specific signs, and begin pairing morphemes with classifiers.
rubric.3: I can produce a small set of taught mouth morphemes on prompted vocabulary, copying the mouth shape along with the sign as it was modeled.
rubric.2: I can copy a mouth morpheme right after a model, but it doesn’t appear with the sign on my own.
rubric.1: I can produce a mouth morpheme only while mirroring a model.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a mouth morpheme even while mirroring.

rubric.4: I can produce common mouth morphemes — size, effort, intensity, distance — paired with the right signs and timed to the movement, including with classifiers.
rubric.3: I can produce taught mouth morphemes for size and distance, along with morphemes tied to specific signs, and I’m beginning to pair morphemes with classifiers.
rubric.2: I can produce a small set of taught morphemes on prompted vocabulary — but size and distance morphemes, sign-specific ones, and classifier pairings only show up with prompting.
rubric.1: I can pair a morpheme with a sign only when cued or drilled.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a mouth morpheme even while mirroring.

rubric.4: I use a wide repertoire of mouth morphemes with native-like timing, layering them over signs and classifiers automatically — and I pick up new morphemes from Deaf signers without instruction.
rubric.3: I can produce common adverbial mouth morphemes — showing size, effort, intensity, and distance — paired with the right signs and timed to the manual movement, including with classifiers.
rubric.2: I can produce taught morphemes with their signs and begin pairing them with classifiers — but my timing is loose, and morphemes drop in spontaneous signing.
rubric.1: I can keep morphemes in my signing only with cueing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a mouth morpheme even while mirroring.

targets.statement: I can keep my mouth ASL-driven instead of mouthing English.

rubric.4: I can reduce my English mouthing as I sign, replacing it with mouth morphemes in practiced structures.
rubric.3: I can sign practiced vocabulary and phrases without mouthing full English sentences.
rubric.2: I can sign short practiced items without full-sentence mouthing when reminded, but English mouthing runs through most of my signing.
rubric.1: I can hold back English mouthing only when cued moment to moment.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I mouth full English sentences even with cueing.

rubric.4: I can keep my mouth activity ASL-driven, using mouth morphemes rather than mouthing English as I sign.
rubric.3: I can reduce my English mouthing as I sign, replacing it with mouth morphemes in practiced structures — though mouthing may return when the language gets hard.
rubric.2: I can sign practiced vocabulary and phrases without mouthing full sentences — but English mouthing still runs through my own signing, and I rarely catch it.
rubric.1: I can replace mouthing with morphemes only when cued.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I mouth full English sentences even with cueing.

rubric.4: My mouth channel is fully ASL — no English mouthing under any pressure, with mouth morphemes doing meaningful work everywhere they belong.
rubric.3: I can keep my mouth activity ASL-driven — using mouth morphemes rather than mouthing English — consistently in my spontaneous signing, not just drilled phrases.
rubric.2: I can replace English mouthing with morphemes in practiced structures — but when the language gets hard, mouthing comes back.
rubric.1: I can keep my mouth ASL-driven only with cueing or rehearsed material.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I mouth full English sentences even with cueing.

standards.name: Classroom Directions
standards.description: Students follow instructions, assignments, and classroom business delivered in ASL, from routine activities to new, unfamiliar projects.

targets.statement: I can follow classroom instructions in ASL.

rubric.4: I can follow multi-step instructions for familiar types of activities and assignments at a steady pace.
rubric.3: I can follow simple one-and-two-step instructions and routine classroom language signed clearly at a slow pace.
rubric.2: I can follow routine one-step instructions, but multi-part directions lose me without a repeat or a demonstration.
rubric.1: I can follow instructions only when they’re repeated, slowed way down, and paired with gestures or demonstrations.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow classroom instructions even with repetition and demonstration.

rubric.4: I can carry out a new, unfamiliar project from ASL instructions alone, explained at a normal pace.
rubric.3: I can follow multi-step instructions for familiar activity and assignment types at a steady pace.
rubric.2: I can follow one-and-two-step instructions and routine classroom language at a slow pace — but multi-step directions at a steady pace still need repeats.
rubric.1: I can follow multi-step instructions only when they’re broken into single steps and repeated for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow classroom instructions even with repetition and demonstration.

rubric.4: I can follow instructions delivered at full speed for complex, multi-stage work — and when classmates are lost, I can re-explain the directions in ASL myself.
rubric.3: I can carry out new, unfamiliar projects and activities from ASL instructions alone — explained at a normal pace, without simplification or repetition — including the expectations and steps.
rubric.2: I can follow multi-step instructions for familiar activity types at a steady pace — but new, unfamiliar projects explained at a normal pace still need repetition or simplification.
rubric.1: I can follow instructions for new projects only when they’re slowed, repeated, or broken into pieces for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow classroom instructions even with repetition and demonstration.

standards.name: Contextual Understanding
standards.description: Students use context to understand: capturing the message, decoding unfamiliar signs from families and parameters, following new classifiers from the established scene, and inferring unstated meaning.

targets.statement: I can capture the message of signed communication.

rubric.4: I can identify the main idea and the key details of signed messages on familiar topics.
rubric.3: I can identify the main point of short, familiar signed messages.
rubric.2: I can pick out familiar signs in a message, but I often miss the main point unless the message is repeated.
rubric.1: I can find the main point only when the message is repeated, slowed, and supported with gestures or visuals.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow a signed message even with repetition and slowed signing.

rubric.4: I can identify the main idea and a high percentage of the details of signed messages, on familiar and less familiar topics.
rubric.3: I can identify the main idea and the key details of signed messages on familiar topics.
rubric.2: I can identify the main point of short, familiar messages — but the key details slip past me.
rubric.1: I can catch the main idea and details only with repetition and slowed signing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow a signed message even with repetition and slowed signing.

rubric.4: I can catch essentially everything — main ideas, details, and implications — from varied signers at full conversational speed, even on new topics, the way I would in my first language.
rubric.3: I can identify the main idea and a high percentage of the details of signed messages on familiar and less familiar topics.
rubric.2: I can identify the main idea and key details on familiar topics — but on less familiar topics, or when the details pile up, my comprehension drops to the gist.
rubric.1: I can capture the details of a message only with repeated viewings or slowed signing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow a signed message even with repetition and slowed signing.

targets.statement: I can decode unfamiliar signs using parameters and sign families.

rubric.4: I can work out the meaning of unfamiliar signs using parameters, sign families, and context.
rubric.3: I can tell apart signs that differ in a single parameter and identify which parameter changed.
rubric.2: I can see that two similar signs are different, but I can’t name which parameter changed or keep the signs apart reliably.
rubric.1: I can spot parameter differences only when the signs are shown side by side, slowly, and the difference is pointed out.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t tell similar signs apart even with help.

rubric.4: I can work out unfamiliar signs from parameters, sign families, and context automatically, as a normal part of comprehension.
rubric.3: I can work out the meaning of unfamiliar signs using parameters, sign families, and context.
rubric.2: I can tell apart signs that differ in a parameter and name what changed — but using parameters, sign families, and context to work out a brand-new sign is still hit-or-miss.
rubric.1: I can work out a new sign’s meaning only with hints, a slowed repeat, or answer choices.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t work out an unfamiliar sign even with hints and repetition.

rubric.4: Unfamiliar signs rarely slow me down: I decode them in real time from form and context alone, and I’m usually right, even in fast signing on new topics.
rubric.3: I can work out the meaning of unfamiliar signs using parameters, sign families, and context as a normal part of comprehension, without losing the thread of the message.
rubric.2: I can work out unfamiliar signs from parameters, sign families, and context when I stop and think — but doing it mid-message, without losing the flow, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can work out a new sign’s meaning only with hints, a slowed repeat, or answer choices.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t work out an unfamiliar sign even with hints and repetition.

targets.statement: I can follow classifiers in signed scenes.

rubric.4: I can identify what a new classifier represents and follow the change in perspective, using the scene and referents already established.
rubric.3: I can connect a classifier to its referent when the referent has just been named.
rubric.2: I can follow a classifier when it’s obvious or acted out, but I lose track of what the handshape stands for.
rubric.1: I can connect a classifier to its referent only when the signer pauses, points back, or re-signs the noun for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t connect a classifier to what it represents even with help.

rubric.4: I can follow new classifiers and shifts in perspective as scenes develop, using the context that has been established.
rubric.3: I can identify what a new classifier represents and follow the perspective change, using the scene and referents already established.
rubric.2: I can connect a classifier to a referent that was just named — but when the referent came earlier in the scene, or the perspective shifts, I lose the thread.
rubric.1: I can follow new classifiers only when the scene is replayed slowly or the referent is re-named.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t connect a classifier to what it represents even with help.

rubric.4: I can track multiple classifiers through long, layered scenes — rapid perspective changes and scale switches included — without losing who or what each handshape represents.
rubric.3: I can follow new classifiers and shifts in perspective as scenes develop, using the established context.
rubric.2: I can identify what a new classifier represents and follow a perspective change in a set-up scene — but as scenes grow and develop, I start losing classifiers and shifts.
rubric.1: I can follow developing scenes only with replays, slowed signing, or someone naming the referents.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t connect a classifier to what it represents even with help.

targets.statement: I can infer meaning that isn’t stated directly.

rubric.4: I can infer meaning that isn’t stated directly, using context and my prior knowledge together.
rubric.3: I can infer meaning that isn’t stated directly when the context makes it clear.
rubric.2: I can understand what’s stated directly, but I take messages at face value — implied meaning gets past me unless someone flags it.
rubric.1: I can find the implied meaning only with guiding questions or a second, slowed viewing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t find implied meaning even with guiding questions.

rubric.4: I read between the lines reliably — tone, implication, the signer’s attitude, and what’s deliberately left unsaid — even on unfamiliar topics.
rubric.3: I can infer meaning that isn’t stated directly, using context and my prior knowledge.
rubric.2: I can infer unstated meaning when the context makes it obvious — but inferences that lean on prior knowledge or subtler cues don’t land consistently.
rubric.1: I can find the implied meaning only with guiding questions or a second, slowed viewing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t find implied meaning even with guiding questions.

standards.name: Grammar
standards.description: Students follow the grammar of signed input: grammatical markers, sentence structure and boundaries, and referents and directional verbs in space.

targets.statement: I can interpret ASL grammatical markers.

rubric.4: I can interpret a wider range of grammatical markers, such as topic and conditional marking, to follow how a sentence is organized.
rubric.3: I can recognize the most common grammatical markers and tell whether a sentence is a statement, a question, or a negation.
rubric.2: I can sometimes tell a question from a statement, but I miss the facial grammar unless it’s exaggerated.
rubric.1: I can tell sentence types apart only when the signer exaggerates the marker and holds it for me.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t tell a question from a statement even with exaggerated marking.

rubric.4: I can interpret the full range of grammatical markers — including subtle ones and several working together — at a normal pace.
rubric.3: I can interpret a wider range of grammatical markers, such as topic and conditional marking, to follow how a sentence is organized.
rubric.2: I can recognize the most common markers — statements, questions, negation — but newer markers like topic and conditional marking slip past me.
rubric.1: I can interpret topic or conditional marking only when it’s pointed out or replayed slowly.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t tell a question from a statement even with exaggerated marking.

rubric.4: I catch grammatical marking the way a fluent signer does — nothing about brow, head, or body grammar escapes me, even in fast, layered signing — and I use it to predict where a sentence is going.
rubric.3: I can interpret the full range of grammatical markers, including subtle ones and several working together, at a normal pace.
rubric.2: I can interpret a wide range of markers in clear signing — but subtle markers, stacked markers, and normal-pace signing still cause misses.
rubric.1: I can interpret subtle or stacked markers only with replays or slowed signing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t tell a question from a statement even with exaggerated marking.

targets.statement: I can follow sentence structure and boundaries.

rubric.4: I can recognize the pauses and boundaries that separate sentences and ideas, and follow sentences that connect two ideas.
rubric.3: I can follow short, simple sentences, recognizing the basic topic-comment setup.
rubric.2: I can follow individual familiar signs, but where one sentence ends and the next begins is mostly a blur.
rubric.1: I can follow a sentence only when it’s signed slowly with exaggerated pauses.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow even short sentences with slowed signing.

rubric.4: I can differentiate ideas across complex, multi-clause sentences, using pauses, boundaries, and sentence structure.
rubric.3: I can recognize the pauses and boundaries that separate sentences and ideas, and I can follow sentences that connect two ideas.
rubric.2: I can follow short, simple topic-comment sentences — but boundaries between sentences and two-idea sentences still blur together.
rubric.1: I can find sentence boundaries only with slowed signing or replays.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow even short sentences with slowed signing.

rubric.4: I can follow any sentence structure in real time — nested clauses, asides, interruptions, and restarts — and long stretches of signing stay clearly organized for me.
rubric.3: I can differentiate ideas across complex, multi-clause sentences, using pauses, boundaries, and sentence structure.
rubric.2: I can follow boundaries between sentences and two-idea sentences — but complex, multi-clause sentences still run together.
rubric.1: I can untangle multi-clause sentences only with replays or slowed signing.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow even short sentences with slowed signing.

targets.statement: I can track referents and directional verbs in space.

rubric.4: I can track a few referents placed in space and the directional verbs that move between them.
rubric.3: I can tell when a signer assigns a person or thing to a location in space and refers back to it.
rubric.2: I can see the signer placing things in space, but when they point back, I’ve lost what the location stands for.
rubric.1: I can follow a spatial reference only when the signer re-names the referent or repeats the setup slowly.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow references to space even with the setup repeated.

rubric.4: I can track referents and directional verbs in space through extended signing, following who acts on whom.
rubric.3: I can track a few referents placed in space and the directional verbs that move between them.
rubric.2: I can tell when a signer sets up a referent and points back to it — but tracking several referents and the verbs moving between them isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can track referents and directional verbs only with replays or a slowed pace.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow references to space even with the setup repeated.

rubric.4: I never lose the map: many referents, rearranged space, and verbs moving between third parties stay clear through a whole story at full speed.
rubric.3: I can track referents and directional verbs in space through extended signing, following who acts on whom.
rubric.2: I can track a few referents and their directional verbs through short stretches — but in extended signing, I lose who acts on whom.
rubric.1: I can follow who acts on whom only with replays, slowed signing, or the referents re-named.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t follow references to space even with the setup repeated.

standards.name: Establishing and Referencing
standards.description: Students use space as a referencing system: establishing referents, indexing back accurately, keeping the spatial setup consistent, and arranging ordered information spatially.

targets.statement: I can establish referents in space and point back to them.

rubric.4: I can establish a few referents at locations and index back to them through short stretches of signing.
rubric.3: I can establish one or two referents at locations in practiced sentences and point back to them within the same short stretch of signing.
rubric.2: I can place a referent at a location, but instead of pointing back to the spot, I usually re-sign the noun.
rubric.1: I can set up and point back to a referent only when the setup is modeled and I’m prompted to index.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t point back to a placed referent even with prompting.

rubric.4: I can establish referents at locations in space and index back to them accurately across extended signing, returning to the same location each time.
rubric.3: I can establish a few referents at locations and index back to them through short stretches of signing.
rubric.2: I can establish one or two referents and point back within the same short stretch — but with more referents in play, my pointing loses precision.
rubric.1: I can keep referents at their locations only with prompting or a planned setup.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t point back to a placed referent even with prompting.

rubric.4: My referencing never wobbles — many referents across a long story, each index landing exactly, even when I deliberately rearrange the space.
rubric.3: I can establish referents at locations in space and index back to them accurately across extended signing, returning to the same location each time rather than pointing vaguely.
rubric.2: I can establish a few referents and index back through short stretches — but across extended signing, my pointing drifts or goes vague.
rubric.1: I can index accurately through longer signing only with planning or reminders.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t point back to a placed referent even with prompting.

targets.statement: I can keep my spatial setup consistent.

rubric.4: I can maintain a consistent spatial setup through a story or explanation, keeping several referents at their locations unless the meaning moves them.
rubric.3: I can maintain my spatial setup through short narratives, though locations may drift in longer or unplanned signing.
rubric.2: I can set referents up, but my locations drift or swap even within a short narrative.
rubric.1: I can hold a setup only with a planned layout or reminders as I go.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my referents lose their locations even with reminders.

rubric.4: My space stays organized through anything — long stories, interruptions, reorganizations — and I deliberately restructure it when the meaning calls for it, without confusing the viewer.
rubric.3: I can maintain a consistent spatial setup through a story or explanation, keeping several referents at their locations unless the meaning moves them.
rubric.2: I can maintain my setup through short narratives — but in longer or unplanned signing, locations still drift or swap.
rubric.1: I can keep a long setup consistent only with planning time or coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my referents lose their locations even with reminders.

targets.statement: I can arrange ordered information spatially.

rubric.4: I can apply taught spatial layouts, such as timelines and steps, to sequence my own information.
rubric.3: I can reproduce taught spatial layouts, such as listing and ranking on my non-dominant hand, as they were modeled.
rubric.2: I can attempt a taught layout, but I need the model in front of me to keep it organized.
rubric.1: I can produce a spatial layout only while copying a model step by step.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t reproduce a layout even while copying.

rubric.4: I can arrange sequences and ordered information spatially — timelines, steps, rankings — so the order is visible.
rubric.3: I can apply taught spatial layouts, such as timelines and steps, to sequence my own information.
rubric.2: I can reproduce taught layouts as modeled — but applying them to organize my own information isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can use a layout for my own content only with prompting and a model nearby.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t reproduce a layout even while copying.

rubric.4: I design my own spatial organization for any content — choosing or inventing the layout that makes order, hierarchy, or comparison clearest — the way fluent signers structure information.
rubric.3: I can arrange sequences and ordered information spatially, laying out timelines, steps, and rankings so the order is visible to my viewer.
rubric.2: I can apply taught layouts to sequence my own information — but choosing the right layout unprompted, and keeping the order visible, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can organize information spatially only with planning time or prompts.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t reproduce a layout even while copying.

standards.name: Agreement & Directionality
standards.description: Students use directional verbs: moving verbs between referents so the path shows who acts on whom, and making directionality a working part of their grammar.

targets.statement: I can move directional verbs between referents.

rubric.4: I can move a growing set of directional verbs between myself and a third referent established in space, showing the path of the action in my own sentences.
rubric.3: I can move a small set of taught directional verbs (GIVE, HELP, ASK) between myself and my conversation partner, showing the direction of the action in practiced sentences.
rubric.2: I can produce taught directional verbs, but the movement doesn’t reliably show who does what to whom.
rubric.1: I can move a verb in the right direction only when it’s modeled and I copy it.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my directional verbs don’t show direction even with a model.

rubric.4: I can move directional verbs between established referents accurately, showing who acts on whom through the verb’s path — including between two referents where I’m not part of the action.
rubric.3: I can move a growing set of directional verbs between myself and a third referent established in space, showing the path of the action in my own sentences.
rubric.2: I can move taught directional verbs between me and my conversation partner — but verbs to or from a third referent set up in space aren’t reliable yet.
rubric.1: I can aim a verb at a third referent only with prompting or a planned setup.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my directional verbs don’t show direction even with a model.

rubric.4: Directional verbs carry my grammar effortlessly — any verb, any pair of referents, chained actions among several parties — always landing at the right locations.
rubric.3: I can move directional verbs between established referents accurately, showing who acts on whom through the verb’s path — including between two referents in space where I’m not part of the action.
rubric.2: I can move directional verbs between myself and a third referent in my own sentences — but verbs between two third-party referents, where I’m not involved, aren’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can show third-party verb paths only with planning or coaching.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or my directional verbs don’t show direction even with a model.

targets.statement: I can use directionality as a working part of my grammar.

rubric.4: I can use directionality with developing consistency in my own sentences, not just practiced ones.
rubric.3: I can change a verb’s direction to change its meaning (I-give-you versus you-give-me).
rubric.2: I can repeat both directions of a verb after a model, but on my own I don’t flip the direction to flip the meaning.
rubric.1: I can change a verb’s direction only when coached through what the new direction means.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t flip a verb’s direction even with coaching.

rubric.4: I can use directionality wherever a verb supports it, modifying verbs for the situation, such as for multiple recipients.
rubric.3: I can use directionality with developing consistency in my own sentences.
rubric.2: I can change a verb’s direction to change its meaning — but in my own sentences, I often fall back on signing the verb plain and naming both people.
rubric.1: I can use directionality in my own sentences only with prompting.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t flip a verb’s direction even with coaching.

rubric.4: Directionality is automatic and inflected for me — I modify paths for number, distribution, and emphasis (each-of-them, all-of-you) without planning it.
rubric.3: I can use directionality wherever a verb supports it, modifying directional verbs for the situation, such as adjusting for multiple recipients.
rubric.2: I can use directionality with developing consistency in my own sentences — but using it everywhere a verb supports it, and modifying for situations like multiple recipients, isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can modify directional verbs for the situation only with prompting or examples.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t flip a verb’s direction even with coaching.

standards.name: Temporal Aspect & Intensity
standards.description: Students modify verb movement to carry meaning about time and degree.

targets.statement: I can modify verb movement to show time and intensity.

rubric.4: I can apply movement modification to familiar verbs in practiced contexts, showing temporal aspect and intensity, and begin pairing the movement change with matching facial expression.
rubric.3: I can produce a small set of taught movement-modified forms as learned units — repeated movement for again-and-again, a sharp movement for intensity — as they were modeled.
rubric.2: I can copy a movement-modified form right after a model, but on my own the modification flattens back to the plain verb.
rubric.1: I can produce a modified form only while mirroring a model.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a movement-modified form even while mirroring.

rubric.4: I can modify verb movement wherever the meaning calls for it, showing temporal aspect, intensity, and degree, with matching non-manual markers.
rubric.3: I can apply movement modification to familiar verbs in practiced contexts, showing temporal aspect and intensity, and I’m beginning to pair the movement change with matching facial expression.
rubric.2: I can produce the taught movement-modified forms as learned units — but applying the pattern to other familiar verbs isn’t there yet.
rubric.1: I can modify a verb’s movement only with a model or prompting.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a movement-modified form even while mirroring.

rubric.4: I inflect movement the way fluent signers do — aspect, degree, and rhythm shaped on the fly, paired seamlessly with non-manual intensity, even on verbs I’ve never seen modified.
rubric.3: I can modify verb movement wherever the meaning calls for it — showing temporal aspect, intensity, and degree — choosing the modification myself and pairing it with matching non-manual markers.
rubric.2: I can modify familiar verbs in practiced contexts with beginning facial pairing — but choosing modifications on my own, wherever meaning calls for them, isn’t consistent yet.
rubric.1: I can choose a movement modification only with prompting or examples.
rubric.0: I haven’t attempted this yet, or I can’t copy a movement-modified form even while mirroring.
